title: 68-year-old man with weight loss and fatigue
candidateInstructions: You are an FY2 doctor in the GP surgery. Mr Geoffrey Barnes, a 68-year-old retired factory worker, has presented with a 3-month history of unintentional weight loss and fatigue. Please take a history to identify the likely cause and plan appropriate investigations.
examinerNotes: This is a suspected lung malignancy scenario. Key features: 40 pack-year smoking history, haemoptysis (volunteered only when asked), weight loss, fatigue, COPD background. Candidate should elicit red flag symptoms, consider lung cancer, and refer urgently via 2-week wait pathway.

patient.name: Geoffrey Barnes
patient.presentingComplaint: I've been losing weight without trying and feeling exhausted all the time for 3 months
patient.reasonForAttendance: My daughter made me come — she says I look like I've lost a lot of weight

hpi.character: Unintentional weight loss, profound fatigue, reduced exercise tolerance
associatedSymptoms: Fatigue — can barely walk to the shops
Persistent cough worse over last 6 weeks
Occasional blood in sputum when coughing
Night sweats once or twice a week
hpi.previousEpisodes: Some breathlessness for years from COPD but this cough and weight loss is new

pmh.notes: Former smoker, had 2 exacerbations last year

socialHistory.occupation: Retired factory worker, 30 years asbestos exposure
socialHistory.occupationalExposure: Significant asbestos exposure in shipyard for 30 years

systemsReview.gastrointestinal: Reduced appetite, no dysphagia, no abdominal pain
systemsReview.neurological: No headache, no confusion, no focal weakness
systemsReview.musculoskeletal: Generalised weakness due to fatigue, no bone pain

examination.respiratory: Reduced air entry right lower zone. Dullness to percussion right base. Bronchial breathing.

investigations.name: Chest X-ray (PA)
investigations.result: 5cm irregular opacity right lower lobe. Right hilar enlargement. Small right pleural effusion. Hyperinflated lung fields consistent with COPD.
investigations.interpretation: Large right lower lobe mass with hilar enlargement and pleural effusion — HIGHLY SUSPICIOUS FOR LUNG CARCINOMA. Urgent CT chest/abdomen/pelvis required.

investigations.name: CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis (staging)
investigations.result: 5.2cm spiculated mass right lower lobe. Ipsilateral hilar and mediastinal lymphadenopathy. Right pleural effusion. Adrenal nodule 2.1cm right side. No brain or bone metastases identified on chest cut.
investigations.interpretation: Findings consistent with right lower lobe lung carcinoma, likely Stage IIIB/IVA given mediastinal nodes and adrenal lesion. Needs MDT discussion and histological confirmation.

investigations.name: Full Blood Count
investigations.interpretation: Microcytic anaemia — likely anaemia of chronic disease or iron deficiency from malignancy. Thrombocytosis — reactive in malignancy.

patient.emotionalState: Stoic and somewhat resigned. Not wanting to cause fuss. Minimises symptoms. Will become visibly worried if doctor mentions cancer.
patient.communicationStyle: Typical retired working man — answers briefly, doesn't volunteer information, slightly defensive. Uses 'a bit' to understate symptoms.
hiddenConcerns: My brother died of lung cancer — I'm terrified it's the same
I don't want to be a burden on my daughter
I keep coughing up blood but I was scared to mention it
doNotVolunteer: Haemoptysis — only if asked 'have you coughed up any blood?'
Night sweats — only if asked about sweating at night
Family history of cancer — only if asked about family health history
Asbestos exposure — only if asked about occupational exposure or his work
The full 8kg weight loss — will say 'a bit' unless pressed for specific amount

primaryDiagnosis: Lung Carcinoma (likely NSCLC — adenocarcinoma or squamous cell)

dataGathering.detailedExpectation: Quantifies weight loss, establishes it is unintentional

dataGathering.criterion: Screens for respiratory red flag symptoms
dataGathering.detailedExpectation: Asks specifically about haemoptysis, cough, dyspnoea, hoarseness
Asks about smoking, calculates or estimates pack-years
Asks about occupational exposures — specifically asbestos

dataGathering.criterion: Orders chest X-ray as first investigation
dataGathering.detailedExpectation: CXR is the initial investigation for suspected lung cancer

clinicalManagement.criterion: Identifies lung cancer as primary concern
clinicalManagement.detailedExpectation: Raises possibility of malignancy or lung cancer
Recognises need for urgent referral to respiratory or oncology
Orders or plans CT chest for staging

interpersonalSkills.detailedExpectation: Raises concern about serious diagnosis sensitively without certainty
Creates opportunity for patient to voice fears

references.source: NICE NG122: Lung cancer — diagnosis and management
NICE NG12: Suspected cancer recognition and referral

learningObjectives: Screen for red flag symptoms of lung malignancy
Elicit relevant occupational and smoking history
